core_behavior.note: All models agree on sum, empty=0, non-integer=error, but none mention null array or null element handling in core behavior.

inputs.note: Model B explicitly lists mixed types including nulls and nested arrays as possible input values; Models A and C describe inputs more generically without enumerating type variants; none explicitly address null array input.

outputs.note: Model B specifies detailed error messages including index and value of the offending element; Models A and C describe error output generically without specifying message format or index information.

assumptions.note: Model B makes explicit assumptions about float-as-integer, boolean treatment, null handling, and single-pass traversal; Models A and C make only general assumptions; none address integer overflow handling explicitly.

failure_modes.note: Model B enumerates specific failure modes per type (float, string, boolean, non-array); Model C mentions null/undefined input and overflow; Model A covers non-array and non-integer generically; null element as a distinct failure mode is absent from A and B's failure_modes field.

edge_cases.note: Model B addresses float-as-integer and boolean edge cases; Model C addresses NaN/Infinity; Model A addresses overflow potential; no model explicitly addresses null array or array containing null as a distinct edge case.

security_constraints.note: Models A and C specify security constraints around input validation and injection prevention; Model B has an empty security_constraints array.

performance_constraints.note: Model B adds concurrency/statelessness constraints; Model C adds memory minimization; Model A focuses on O(n) and graceful degradation; the models introduce different performance dimensions not specified in the requirement.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 5 of the 8 fields.
